explanationBullets: 12-lead ECG with uniform wide QRS complexes across all leads
Every QRS complex shares the same morphology and axis
Regular R-R intervals throughout the tracing
Positive concordance visible in precordial leads (all QRS deflections in the same direction)
Wide complex tachycardia with fusion beats visible (narrower complexes mixed in)
Fusion beats occur when a sinus impulse partially captures the ventricle during VT
The predominant QRS morphology is uniform: identical shape beat to beat
Fusion and capture beats confirm ventricular origin (not SVT with aberrancy)
Precordial leads (V1 through V6) showing monomorphic VT
Each lead shows a consistent, repeating QRS morphology
Wide QRS complexes with no beat-to-beat variation in shape or axis
AV dissociation may be present but QRS shape remains uniform
Lead II rhythm strip showing monomorphic VT at a rate of 192 bpm
Uniform QRS morphology: every complex is identical
Regular R-R intervals with no variation in amplitude
Wide QRS complexes with a single consistent waveform pattern
12-lead ECG showing monomorphic VT during hemodynamic compromise
All QRS complexes share the same morphology across every lead
Regular rate with uniform, wide QRS complexes
Consistent axis and amplitude throughout the tracing
Lead II showing wide complex tachycardia with uniform QRS morphology
Each beat has the same shape: tall upright deflection with consistent width
No variation in QRS axis or amplitude from beat to beat
Shows the classic "short-long-short" initiating sequence for Torsades de Pointes
After initiation, QRS morphology varies continuously from beat to beat
Progressively changing QRS axis and amplitude as the arrhythmia develops
No two consecutive complexes share the same morphology
Torsades de Pointes with prolonged QT interval visible before onset
QRS complexes continuously change in morphology, axis, and amplitude
Classic "twisting of the points" pattern around the isoelectric line
Wide, chaotic complexes with no consistent bundle branch block pattern
Lead II strip showing polymorphic VT with beat-to-beat QRS variation
QRS axis and morphology change continuously across the tracing
Irregular R-R intervals with no repeating pattern
Complexes vary in both direction and amplitude, unlike monomorphic VT
Torsades de Pointes recorded by an implantable defibrillator (AICD)
Sinusoidal variation in QRS amplitude: waxing and waning pattern
QRS morphology changes continuously, no two beats are identical
Straight line near the end represents the device shock converting the rhythm
12-lead ECG showing Torsades de Pointes with onset from sinus rhythm
Precordial leads show continuously varying QRS morphology and axis
Sinusoidal amplitude modulation visible across multiple leads
Rhythm strip at bottom confirms the polymorphic, twisting pattern
Classic Torsades de Pointes with sinusoidal waxing and waning of QRS amplitude
QRS complexes appear to rotate around the baseline
Amplitude increases then decreases in a smooth envelope pattern
No consistent QRS morphology from beat to beat
Torsades de Pointes with dramatic variation in QRS amplitude and axis
QRS complexes twist above and below the baseline in a spinning pattern
Amplitude modulation creates the characteristic spindle-shaped envelope
Completely irregular rhythm with no repeating QRS morphology
Polymorphic VT with continuously varying QRS morphology
Each complex differs from its neighbors in shape, width, and amplitude
No consistent axis: complexes shift between upright and inverted
12-lead ECG with fast, uniform, wide QRS complexes across all leads
Sinusoidal-appearing waveform with identical morphology beat to beat
Regular R-R intervals at a very rapid rate
Consistent QRS axis and amplitude throughout the tracing
Rhythm strip showing wide complex tachycardia with uniform QRS morphology
Every beat is identical in shape: deep downward deflection with consistent width
Regular R-R intervals with no variation
No beat-to-beat change in axis or amplitude
Lead II showing monomorphic VT with labeled fusion (F) and capture (C) beats
Fusion beats are narrower complexes where sinus and VT impulses merge
Capture beats are narrow QRS complexes from a conducted sinus beat during VT
Fusion and capture beats confirm ventricular origin and AV dissociation
12-lead ECG from a 75-year-old showing monomorphic VT
Wide QRS complexes with uniform morphology across all leads
LBBB-type morphology suggesting right ventricular origin
Regular rate with no beat-to-beat variation in QRS shape
RBBB morphology in V1 (tall R wave) indicates LV origin
Uniform QRS complexes with identical shape in each lead
Wide QRS with consistent axis throughout the tracing
LBBB morphology in V1 (deep S wave) indicates RV origin
All QRS complexes share the same monomorphic pattern
Regular rate with no variation in QRS morphology between beats
V1 lead showing monomorphic VT with annotated fusion (F) and capture (C) beats
The predominant wide QRS complexes all share the same morphology
Narrower fusion beats and a capture beat prove AV dissociation
Uniform QRS shape between the fusion/capture beats confirms monomorphic VT
Lead V1 showing VT with retrograde Wenckebach conduction (ladder diagram below)
Uniform wide QRS complexes with identical morphology throughout
Red arrows mark retrograde P waves conducted back to the atria
Despite retrograde conduction, QRS morphology remains monomorphic
Rhythm strip showing wide complex tachycardia with uniform QRS morphology
Each complex has an identical shape and width
Regular R-R intervals throughout the strip
Consistent upright-then-downward deflection pattern in every beat
Lead II rhythm strip showing monomorphic VT with sinusoidal-appearing uniform QRS
Wide, smooth, rounded complexes with identical shape beat to beat
Regular rate with no variation in amplitude or axis
No P waves visible: the ventricles drive the rhythm independently
12-lead ECG showing onset of Torsades de Pointes from sinus rhythm with prolonged QT
Initial sinus beats show markedly prolonged QT interval
VT onset shows continuously changing QRS morphology and axis
Precordial leads demonstrate the polymorphic, twisting pattern of TdP
Rhythm strip showing polymorphic VT with continuously varying QRS morphology
QRS amplitude waxes and wanes across the tracing
No two consecutive complexes share the same shape or axis
Irregular R-R intervals with no repeating pattern
Torsades de Pointes with clear transition from sinus rhythm
Initial beats show a triggering PVC, then polymorphic VT develops
QRS complexes vary dramatically in amplitude and direction
Classic waxing-waning spindle pattern characteristic of TdP

Wide QRS complexes with uniform LBBB-type morphology across all leads
Very rapid rate with regular R-R intervals
Identical QRS shape beat to beat with no variation in axis or amplitude

Wide QRS complexes with consistent morphology in each lead
Uniform QRS axis and shape throughout the tracing
No beat-to-beat variation in QRS width, amplitude, or direction
Wide QRS complexes with a single repeating morphology
Uniform axis across limb and precordial leads
Regular rate with identical QRS shape in every beat
WaveMaven Case #274: 12-lead ECG with classic fast monomorphic VT
Rapid, wide QRS complexes with identical morphology beat to beat
Regular R-R intervals at a high rate
Negative concordance in precordial leads (all QRS deflections downward in V1-V6)
WaveMaven Case #290: Monomorphic VT in the setting of acute STEMI
Wide, uniform QRS complexes with consistent morphology
All beats share the same shape despite underlying ischemia
Regular rate with no beat-to-beat variation in QRS axis
WaveMaven Case #343: 12-lead ECG showing ventricular tachycardia
Wide QRS complexes with uniform morphology across all leads
Consistent QRS shape and axis throughout the tracing
AV dissociation likely present with independent atrial activity
WaveMaven Case #395: 12-lead ECG showing monomorphic VT
Uniform wide QRS complexes with identical beat-to-beat morphology
Consistent axis and amplitude in each lead
Regular rhythm with no variation in QRS shape
WaveMaven Case #403: 12-lead ECG with fast monomorphic VT
Sinusoidal-appearing wide QRS complexes at a very rapid rate
Every complex is identical in morphology across all leads
Regular R-R intervals with uniform amplitude and axis
WaveMaven Case #502: 12-lead ECG showing wide complex tachycardia
Uniform QRS morphology with the same shape in every beat
Wide QRS complexes consistent with ventricular origin
No variation in axis or amplitude from beat to beat
WaveMaven Case #314: 12-lead ECG showing polymorphic VT
QRS morphology varies between beats across multiple leads
Changing QRS axis and amplitude from complex to complex
No consistent bundle branch block pattern: complexes shift in shape and direction
Atrial fibrillation with LBBB: wide QRS mimics VT, but the rhythm is irregularly irregular
Irregular R-R intervals are the key clue: VT is almost always regular
LBBB morphology is consistent in every beat (rSR\' pattern, not bizarre)
No AV dissociation, no fusion or capture beats
SVT with RBBB aberrancy in a 44-year-old male
Wide QRS with classic rsR\' in V1 consistent with RBBB, not VT
Regular, narrow-appearing P waves may be visible before each QRS
Typical RBBB pattern (V1 rsR\', V6 wide S) favors supraventricular origin
Pre-excited atrial fibrillation (WPW + AFib) in a 29-year-old
Bizarre, wide QRS complexes that look like VT, but the rhythm is irregularly irregular
Varying QRS morphology from beat to beat due to different degrees of pre-excitation
Very fast rate with some extremely short R-R intervals (danger of VF if AV node blockers given)
Sinus tachycardia with wide QRS (bundle branch block)
P waves visible before each QRS complex, with a consistent PR interval
Wide QRS due to conduction delay, not ventricular origin
Rate is appropriate for sinus tachycardia; P-wave axis is normal
Atrial fibrillation with RVR and Ashman phenomenon (RBBB aberrancy)
Irregularly irregular baseline rhythm confirms AFib, not VT
Wide beats follow short-long-short R-R cycle (Ashman phenomenon)
Aberrant beats have RBBB morphology, not the bizarre shapes typical of VT
Regular rhythm at a very fast rate, but QRS complexes are not truly wide
No AV dissociation; the atria and ventricles are linked 1:1
Absence of wide, bizarre QRS morphology argues against VT
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Irregularly irregular rhythm is the hallmark of AFib, not VT
QRS complexes vary in timing but not in fundamental morphology
No fusion beats, no capture beats, no AV dissociation
Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) pattern with sinus rhythm and delta waves
Short PR interval (<120ms) with slurred upstroke (delta wave) on QRS
Wide QRS is due to ventricular pre-excitation via accessory pathway, not VT
During tachycardia, this pattern produces wide complexes that closely mimic VT
Regular rhythm that can mimic VT, but flutter waves (sawtooth) visible in inferior leads
QRS complexes are conducted from the atria, not generated in the ventricles
Carotid sinus massage or adenosine would unmask the flutter waves
Antidromic AVRT (WPW) in a 15-year-old: regular broad complex tachycardia at ~200 bpm
Wide, bizarre QRS with LBBB morphology from anterograde conduction via accessory pathway
Looks identical to VT, but the patient is young with no structural heart disease
Previous ECG in sinus rhythm showed delta waves and short PR confirming WPW
Pre-excited atrial fibrillation (WPW + AFib): wide, irregular, and dangerous
Irregularly irregular rhythm with bizarre wide QRS complexes at very fast rates
Varying QRS width reflects different degrees of pre-excitation per beat
AV node blockers (adenosine, verapamil, digoxin) are contraindicated: may cause VF
Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) with narrow QRS complexes
Regular rhythm at a fast rate, but QRS is narrow (<120ms)
Narrow QRS confirms supraventricular origin with normal ventricular conduction
VT produces wide QRS (>120ms); narrow complex rules it out immediately
Irregularly irregular rhythm with wide, bizarre QRS complexes
Irregular R-R intervals distinguish this from monomorphic VT
Accessory pathway conducts AF directly to ventricles, bypassing AV node
Regular wide complex tachycardia that mimics VT
Sawtooth flutter waves may be visible in inferior leads or V1
LBBB pattern (rS in V1, broad R in V6) is consistent across all beats
WaveMaven #88: AFib with transient RBBB aberrancy
Irregularly irregular baseline confirms atrial fibrillation, not VT
Some beats conducted with RBBB aberrancy (wider QRS after longer pauses)
Ashman phenomenon: aberrant conduction follows a long-short R-R sequence
WaveMaven #111: AFib with RVR in a patient on flecainide
The clinical question asks: "Is this VT?" The answer is no.
Flecainide widens QRS by slowing sodium channel conduction
Irregularly irregular rhythm confirms AFib despite the very wide QRS
Wide QRS complexes from LBBB can mimic VT morphology
Irregular R-R intervals confirm AFib as the underlying rhythm
Consistent LBBB morphology in every beat (not bizarre, not varying)
Regular wide complex tachycardia from supraventricular origin
Typical RBBB pattern: rsR\' in V1 with wide S wave in lateral leads
RBBB morphology with normal axis favors SVT over VT
WaveMaven #197: Atrial tachycardia with 2:1 block and LBBB
Wide QRS from LBBB with a rapid atrial rate conducted 2:1
P waves may be visible at twice the ventricular rate
LBBB morphology is consistent and typical, not bizarre
WaveMaven #258: Atrial flutter with bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB)
Wide QRS from bifascicular block, not ventricular origin
Flutter waves may be visible at 300/min with variable conduction
RBBB + left axis deviation pattern is consistent with conduction disease
WaveMaven #512: Pre-excited AFib (WPW), presented as a "stat" wide complex tachycardia
Irregular wide complexes with varying QRS width from beat to beat
Irregularity rules out monomorphic VT; varying pre-excitation rules out PMVT
Extremely rapid rate via accessory pathway; AV node blockers contraindicated
